figures.caption: Fig. 1. Real-world demonstration using TacLoc. A GelSight Mini sensor touches a textureless 3D-printed object (left), producing a tactile image that is converted into a dense point cloud and registered to a prior CAD model (right). The zoomed view shows the tactile point cloud (blue) aligned on the CAD model (red).

Fig. 8. Parameter analysis on two YCB-Reg objects. A longer sliding length enhances the performance of tactile localization, aligning with human intuition. Additionally, the accuracy of the end-effector pose is critical: a more precise end-effector pose results in a more consistent submap, thereby improving the performance of TacLoc.
Fig. 11. Per-stage computational profile. FPFH-based initial data association and transformation verification are the most time-intensive stages.

Fig. 9. Experimental results demonstrate the efficacy of normal-guided pruning. (a) Graph density, number of cliques, and computation time decrease as δα varies; (b) The number of edges and computation time exhibit an approximately polynomial relationship. When δα is 30◦ or 180◦, the average number of edges and computation times are 7, 473.82 edges with 0.20s and 15, 476.75 edges with 2.87s, respectively.

Fig. 2. Overview of the TacLoc pipeline for one-shot global tactile localization. The process starts with feature extraction to establish initial correspondences. A compatibility graph is constructed based on distance and normal consistency, and maximal cliques are identified for pose hypothesis generation. Finally, a hypothesis-and-verification approach is applied to refine the pose estimation, achieving partial-to-full tactile localization. It is worth noting that a CAD model is indispensable as a prior map and end-effector poses are required to build submaps during sliding touch.
